questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of INNOCUOUS:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: monitoring / technology / improved / has / safety / mine
options: Improved technology has mine safety monitoring
Monitoring mine safety has technology improved
Technology has improved mine safety monitoring
Technology has improved mine safety monitoring
Mine safety monitoring technology has improved
questions.solution: Technology has improved mine safety monitoring

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of BELLIGERENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: data / protect / essential / is / privacy / to
options: Essential is to protect data privacy
Data privacy is essential to protect

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PRUDENT:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: education / the / to / key / is / success
options: Education is the key to success
To success the key is education
Success is the key to education
Education is the key to success
questions.solution: Education is the key to success

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PRAGMATIC:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: mining / India / in / industry / is / a / important / sector
options: An important sector in India is mining industry
Mining industry is a important sector in India
Mining is an important sector in India
In India mining is important sector
Mining is an important sector in India
questions.solution: Mining is an important sector in India

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of ALLEVIATE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: team / successfully / the / completed / project / the
options: Successfully the team completed the project
The team successfully completed the project
The project successfully completed the team
Completed the project the team successfully
The team successfully completed the project
questions.solution: The team successfully completed the project

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of PLACID:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: performance / regular / improves / employee / training

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of VENERATE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: climate / urgent / change / requires / action / addressing
options: Addressing climate change requires urgent action
Climate change addressing urgent action requires
Addressing climate change requires urgent action
Requires urgent action change addressing climate
Urgent action climate change requires addressing
questions.solution: Addressing climate change requires urgent action

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of TENACIOUS:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: quality / compromising / without / costs / reduce / should
options: Costs should reduce without compromising quality
Quality compromising without reduce costs should
Costs should reduce without compromising quality
Compromising quality without costs should reduce
Should reduce costs without compromising quality
questions.solution: Costs should reduce without compromising quality

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of MUNDANE:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: P: The automobile industry is shifting towards electric vehicles. Q: Battery technology has improved significantly in recent years. R: Charging infrastructure is being expanded rapidly. S: This transition will reduce carbon emissions.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Correct: She is smarter than any student in the class.
options: She is the smartest than any student in the class.
She is smarter than any other student in the class.
She is smarter than any student in the class.
She is smarter than all student in the class.
questions.solution: She is smarter than any other student in the class.

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The manager ____ the proposal before the board.

questions.stem: Find the error: The professor (A) / gave a (B) / one-hour (C) / lecture. (D)

questions.stem: The mineral reserves ____ estimated at 500 million tonnes.

questions.stem: Find the error: Each of (A) / the participants (B) / were given (C) / a certificate. (D)

questions.stem: Antonym of SURREPTITIOUS:

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of INTREPID:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about a singly linked list is correct?
options: Its size must be fixed at creation
It does not support O(1) random access by index
questions.solution: Linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach an arbitrary index — no random access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Thrashing in a virtual-memory system refers to:
options: Excessive paging that cripples useful work
questions.solution: When processes spend more time swapping pages than computing, the system thrashes.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interpreted as an 8-bit two's-complement number, the bit pattern 11111010 equals:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A pointer variable stores:
options: The memory address of another variable
questions.solution: Pointers hold addresses, enabling indirect access and dynamic structures.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 1 Mbps has a round-trip time of 50 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Bubble sort has a worst-case time complexity of:
questions.solution: Bubble sort makes ~n²/2 comparisons in the worst case.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Compared with a process, a thread:
options: Shares the address space of its process
Has its own copy of global data
Has a completely separate address space
questions.solution: Threads of a process share code, data and heap, but have their own stack and registers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A data word of 48 bits occupies how many bytes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A primary key in a relation must be:
questions.solution: A primary key uniquely identifies each tuple and cannot be null.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing compilation and interpretation is correct?
options: Compiled code generally runs faster than interpreted code
questions.solution: Ahead-of-time compiled native code typically executes faster than line-by-line interpretation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 200×80 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Dijkstra's algorithm finds shortest paths correctly only when edge weights are:
questions.solution: Dijkstra assumes non-negative weights; negative edges require Bellman–Ford.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the graph shown (edges A–B, A–C, B–D, C–D), a breadth-first search from A, visiting neighbours alphabetically, gives the order:
questions.solution: BFS from A visits A, then its neighbours B and C, then D: A, B, C, D.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A relation in BCNF is also in 3NF, but the converse may fail when:
options: A non-key attribute determines part of a candidate key
questions.solution: BCNF is stricter: every determinant must be a superkey; 3NF allows certain prime-attribute dependencies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is correct?
options: Any Boolean function can be built using only NAND gates
questions.solution: NAND (and NOR) are the universal gates; AND/OR/XOR alone are not functionally complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Binary search on a sorted array of 128 elements needs at most how many comparisons?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Translates the whole program before execution
Executes line by line at run time
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A link of 100 Mbps has a round-trip time of 100 ms. The bandwidth-delay product is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: To find shortest paths when some edge weights are negative (but no negative cycle), use:
questions.solution: Bellman–Ford handles negative edges and detects negative cycles; Dijkstra cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An index on a database column generally:
options: Speeds up reads but can slow down writes
Speeds up both reads and writes equally
questions.solution: Indexes accelerate lookups but add overhead to inserts/updates that must maintain them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 16K memory locations?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The ACID properties of a transaction stand for:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the binary number 1011₂ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The portion of code where a process accesses shared resources is the:
questions.solution: Mutual exclusion must be enforced over the critical section to avoid race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which condition is NOT one of the four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.solution: Deadlock needs mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, NO preemption, and circular wait — so 'preemption' is not a condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The maximum number of comparisons made by bubble sort on 10 elements is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Belady's anomaly — more frames causing more page faults — can occur with which replacement policy?
questions.solution: FIFO can exhibit Belady's anomaly; stack algorithms like LRU and OPT cannot.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 3, push 8, push 12, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">25</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">3</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Every recursive function must have a:
questions.solution: Without a base (terminating) case, recursion never stops and overflows the stack.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAM is described as volatile memory because it:
options: Loses its contents when power is removed
questions.solution: Volatile memory needs power to keep data; RAM clears on power-off.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 9, push 12, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 12 then pop (removes 12), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">9</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">5</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two-phase locking (2PL) guarantees:
questions.solution: 2PL (growing then shrinking phase) ensures serializability, though deadlocks are still possible.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: With a page size of 8 KB, the number of bits used for the page offset is:
questions.solution: 8KB = 2^13 bytes, so the offset field is 13 bits.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A queue follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: Elements leave a queue in the same order they entered — FIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A problem that is NP-complete is:
options: In NP and at least as hard as every NP problem
Solvable in polynomial time for sure
questions.solution: NP-complete = in NP and NP-hard; a polynomial solution to one would solve all of NP.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 40), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 12, 20, 28, 40, 65.

figure.caption: Binary search tree

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache memory is used because it is:
options: Faster than main memory, reducing access time
questions.solution: Cache is small, fast memory holding frequently used data near the CPU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An 10-bit register can represent how many distinct values?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement is INCORRECT?
options: Binary search runs in O(log n)
Binary search works on an unsorted array
Linear search works on unsorted arrays
Binary search requires a sorted array
questions.solution: Binary search only works on sorted data — the claim that it works unsorted is false.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Naive recursive Fibonacci fib(7) (with fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1) makes how many calls to the base cases fib(0) or fib(1)?
questions.solution: The number of base-case calls for fib(n) equals fib(n+1) = 21, illustrating exponential blow-up.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting n elements into a dynamic array that doubles on resize has an amortized insertion cost of:
questions.solution: Doubling makes total work O(n) over n inserts, i.e. amortized O(1) each.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A foreign key enforces:
questions.solution: A foreign key must match a primary key in the referenced table (or be null), enforcing referential integrity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore is primarily used for:
questions.solution: Semaphores coordinate access to shared resources, preventing race conditions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the OR gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 0, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: DNS is responsible for:
options: Translating domain names to IP addresses
questions.solution: The Domain Name System resolves human-readable names to IP addresses.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 500 MB at 250 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The NAND and NOR gates are called universal gates because they can:
questions.solution: Any logic function can be built using only NAND (or only NOR) gates.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In 8-bit two's complement, the range of representable integers is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 6 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Borrowing 3 bits from the host portion of a class-C network yields how many subnets, each with how many usable hosts?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: UDP, in contrast to TCP, is:
questions.solution: UDP sends datagrams without setup, acknowledgements or guaranteed delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A router operates principally at the OSI:
questions.solution: Routers forward packets using IP addresses at the network layer.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Inserting a node at the front of a singly linked list takes time:
questions.solution: Only a couple of pointer updates are needed — constant time.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An ideal 4-stage pipeline processes 1000 instructions. The speedup over a non-pipelined CPU is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Normalization of a database is performed mainly to:
options: Reduce redundancy and avoid update anomalies
questions.solution: Normalization organizes data to minimize duplication and anomalies.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The OSI reference model has how many layers?
questions.solution: OSI: Physical, Data-link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 1F₁₆ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In an n-bit two's-complement representation, the number of distinct values is:
questions.solution: n bits encode 2ⁿ distinct values, from −2ⁿ⁻¹ to 2ⁿ⁻¹ − 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The truth table shown (output Y) corresponds to which logic function of A and B?
questions.solution: Reading the output column identifies the function as XOR.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In round-robin CPU scheduling, each process is given:
options: A fixed time quantum in turn
questions.solution: Round-robin cycles through ready processes, each running for one time quantum.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Searching a skewed (degenerate) binary search tree of n nodes takes, in the worst case:
questions.solution: A fully skewed BST behaves like a linked list, giving O(n) search.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Transferring 100 MB at 50 MB/s takes about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A flip-flop is a circuit that can store:
questions.solution: A flip-flop is a basic 1-bit memory element.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which comparison of sorting algorithms is correct?
options: Merge sort is stable; standard quicksort is not
Quicksort is stable; merge sort is not
questions.solution: Merge sort preserves equal-key order (stable); typical quicksort does not.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Accessing the i-th element of an array by index takes time:
questions.solution: Arrays support constant-time random access via base address + index×size.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the NOR gate shown with inputs A = 0 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about greedy algorithms is correct?
options: They do not always yield a globally optimal solution
They are the same as dynamic programming
questions.solution: Greedy choices are optimal only for problems with the greedy-choice property (e.g. MST); not in general.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 50×20 array of 8-byte elements occupies how much memory?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which logic gate outputs 1 only when all inputs are 1?
questions.solution: AND gives 1 only when every input is 1.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paging is a memory-management scheme that maps:
questions.solution: Paging divides memory into fixed-size pages/frames and uses a page table to map them.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A hash table with 50 slots holds 30 keys. Its load factor is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of quicksort is:
questions.solution: With consistently poor pivots (e.g. already-sorted input) quicksort degrades to O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the binary search tree shown (root 60), the in-order traversal sequence is:
questions.solution: In-order (left–root–right) of a BST gives ascending order: 37, 45, 50, 60, 85.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: B-trees are preferred over binary search trees for on-disk databases because they:
options: Are shallow (high fan-out), reducing disk accesses
questions.solution: High branching factor keeps B-trees shallow, minimizing expensive disk seeks.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The decimal value of the hexadecimal number 40₁₆ is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quicksort attains its O(n²) worst case when:
options: The pivot is always the median
The array is large but random
The pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
questions.solution: Maximally unbalanced partitions (e.g. sorted input with first/last pivot) give O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A perfect binary tree of height 5 (root at height 1) has how many nodes?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The well-known TCP port for unencrypted HTTP is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TCP provides a service that is:
questions.solution: TCP uses handshakes, acknowledgements and retransmission for reliable, ordered delivery.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 10 ns, memory access = 200 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.9 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A /29 IPv4 subnet provides how many usable host addresses?

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement about virtual memory is INCORRECT?
options: A page fault may load a page from disk
Virtual memory can exceed physical RAM
Page faults are handled entirely by the CPU without disk access
The page table maps pages to frames
questions.solution: A page fault for a non-resident page requires the OS to fetch it from disk.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The SQL clause used to retrieve data from a table is:
questions.solution: SELECT … FROM … queries rows from one or more tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 9, push 20, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A switch (LAN) forwards frames using:
options: MAC addresses at the data-link layer
Port numbers at the transport layer
IP addresses at the network layer
questions.solution: A Layer-2 switch learns and forwards by MAC address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm is used for deadlock:
questions.solution: It checks whether granting a request leaves the system in a safe state (avoidance).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A stack follows the ordering principle:
questions.solution: The most recently pushed element is the first to be popped — LIFO.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 18 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
At program load time, all at once
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 8K memory locations?
